request.question: When was the winner of the series of international poker tournaments started in 2002 by Steven Lipscomb who has also won four World Series of Poker bracelets born?

title: World Series of Poker Europe
World Series of Poker bracelet
World Poker Tour
World Series of Poker Africa
World Series of Poker Asia Pacific
Gene Fisher
Phil Hellmuth
John Hennigan (poker player)
Michael Gathy
World Poker Tour bracelet
sentences: The World Series of Poker Europe (WSOPE) is the first expansion effort of World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.
Since 1970, participants had to travel to Las Vegas if they wanted to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).
Although the WSOP held circuit events in other locations, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.
The inaugural WSOPE, held in 2007, marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside of Las Vegas.

From its inception to the 2013 tournament, players from 19 countries — USA (10), France (4), UK (3), Denmark (3), Canada (2), Norway (2), Portugal (2), Italy (2), Afghanistan, Germany, Indonesia, Spain, New Zealand, Sweden, Tunisia, Switzerland, Australia, Italy and Finland — have won bracelets.
The World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet is considered the most coveted non-monetary prize a poker player can win.
Since 1976, a bracelet has been awarded to the winner of every event at the annual WSOP.
Even if the victory occurred before 1976, WSOP championships are now counted as "bracelets".
During the first years of the WSOP only a handful of bracelets were awarded each year.
In 1990, there were only 14 bracelet events.
By 2000, that number increased to 24.
As the popularity of poker has increased during the 2000s, the number of events has likewise increased.
In 2011, 58 bracelets were awarded at the WSOP, seven at the World Series of Poker Europe (WSOPE), and one to the WSOP National Circuit Champion.
This brought the total number of bracelets awarded up to 959.
Five additional bracelets were awarded for the first time in April 2013 at the inaugural World Series of Poker Asia-Pacific (WSOP APAC) in Melbourne, Australia.
In 2017, 74 for bracelets were awarded at the WSOP and an additional 11 will be awarded at the WSOPE in Czech Republic.
The World Poker Tour (WPT) is a series of international poker tournaments and associated television series broadcasting the final table of each tournament.

It was started in 2002 in the United States by attorney/television producer Steven Lipscomb, who served as CEO of WPT Enterprises (WPTE), the firm that controlled the World Poker Tour up to November 2009.
In 2008, the WPT started offering bracelets to its event champions.
Players who won a title prior to the release of the WPT Bracelet were given one retroactively.
The World Series of Poker Africa (WSOPA) is the second expansion effort of World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.
Since 1970, participants had to travel to Las Vegas if they wanted to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).
Although the WSOP started holding circuit events in 2005 in other states, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.
In 2007, the inaugural World Series of Poker Europe marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside of Las Vegas.
In 2010, the WSOP expanded overseas once again, only this time to Gauteng, South Africa.
While the WSOPE awarded bracelets, the WSOPA was considered a circuit event with the winners receiving Championship Rings instead of bracelets.
The WSOPA did not occur in 2011, but the series of tournaments resumed in 2012.
The World Series of Poker Asia Pacific (WSOP APAC) is the third expansion of the World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.
Since 1970 participants have had to travel to Las Vegas, Nevada to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).
Although the WSOP started holding circuit events in 2005 in other states, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.
In 2007 the inaugural World Series of Poker Europe marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside Las Vegas.
The WSOP further expanded to Africa in 2010 and 2012 however these events did not award any bracelets.
Gene Fisher is an American poker player from El Paso, Texas who won World Series of Poker bracelets in 1980 and 1993, winning the same amount ($113,400) in both events.
He has also cashed in various other poker tournaments.
Fisher stopped playing in the World Series of Poker for many years due to problems with the IRS.
Phillip Jerome Hellmuth Jr. (born July 16, 1964) is an American professional poker player who has won a record fourteen World Series of Poker bracelets.

He is the winner of the Main Event of the 1989 World Series of Poker (WSOP) and the Main Event of the 2012 World Series of Poker Europe (WSOPE), and he is a 2007 inductee of the WSOP's Poker Hall of Fame.
Hellmuth is also known for his temperamental "poker brat" personality.

John Hennigan (born August 10, 1970) is an American professional poker player from Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, who, in his career, has won four World Series of Poker bracelets and a World Poker Tour (WPT) title.
Michael Gathy is a professional poker player who won World Series of Poker bracelets at the 2012 and 2013 World Series of Poker.
He was the first World Series turbo event winner and the second Belgian bracelet winner.
He is one of two Belgians with multiple bracelets.
The World Poker Tour bracelet is the World Poker Tour's (WPT) answer to the World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet.
Beginning in 1976, the WSOP started awarding bracelets to winners of WSOP events.
The WSOP bracelet has become synonymous with greatness.

"It's impossible to overstate the value of a World Series of Poker gold bracelet to anyone who takes the game seriously," stated World Series of Poker Commissioner Jeffrey Pollack during the 2006 bracelet unveiling.
"It is the equivalent of winning the Stanley Cup in hockey or the Lombardi Trophy in football."
Since their introduction, a poker player's success has been measured by the number of bracelets they had won.
With introduction of the WPT bracelet, the WPT hopes to capitalize on the prestige of winning poker bracelets.

WPT Founder, President and CEO, Steve Lipscomb said, "The championship bracelet has become synonymous with poker as a symbol of achievement and respect, and we are honored to continue the tradition that Benny Binion [the founder of the WSOP] began over 30 years ago."

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: When was the winner of the series of international poker tournaments started in 2002 by Steven Lipscomb who has also won four World Series of Poker bracelets born?
hop1_docs: World Poker Tour | The World Poker Tour (WPT) is a series of international poker tournaments and associated television series broadcasting the final table of each tournament.  It was started in 2002 in the United States by attorney/television producer Steven Lipscomb, who served as CEO of WPT Enterprises (WPTE), the firm that controlled the World Poker Tour up to November 2009.  In 2008, the WPT started offering bracelets to its event champions.  Players who won a title prior to the release of the WPT Bracelet were given one retroactively.

World Poker Tour bracelet | The World Poker Tour bracelet is the World Poker Tour's (WPT) answer to the World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelet.  Beginning in 1976, the WSOP started awarding bracelets to winners of WSOP events.  The WSOP bracelet has become synonymous with greatness.  "It's impossible to overstate the value of a World Series of Poker gold bracelet to anyone who takes the game seriously," stated World Series of Poker Commissioner Jeffrey Pollack during the 2006 bracelet unveiling.  "It is the equivalent of winning the Stanley Cup in hockey or the Lombardi Trophy in football."  Since their introduction, a poker player's success has been measured by the number of bracelets they had won.  With introduction of the WPT bracelet, the WPT hopes to capitalize on the prestige of winning poker bracelets.  WPT Founder, President and CEO, Steve Lipscomb said, "The championship bracelet has become synonymous with poker as a symbol of achievement and respect, and we are honored to continue the tradition that Benny Binion [the founder of the WSOP] began over 30 years ago."

David Chiu (poker player) | David Chiu (; born August 23, 1960 in Nanning, China) is a Chinese American professional poker player, based in Las Vegas, Nevada, who has won five World Series of Poker bracelets.  He is also the winner of the 2008 World Poker Tour's WPT World Championship, and the first winner of the Tournament of Champions of Poker.

World Series of Poker Africa | The World Series of Poker Africa (WSOPA) is the second expansion effort of World Series of Poker-branded poker tournaments outside the United States.  Since 1970, participants had to travel to Las Vegas if they wanted to compete in the World Series of Poker (WSOP).  Although the WSOP started holding circuit events in 2005 in other states, the main tournaments, which awarded bracelets to the winners, were exclusively held in Las Vegas.  In 2007, the inaugural World Series of Poker Europe marked the first time that a WSOP bracelet was awarded outside of Las Vegas.  In 2010, the WSOP expanded overseas once again, only this time to Gauteng, South Africa.  While the WSOPE awarded bracelets, the WSOPA was considered a circuit event with the winners receiving Championship Rings instead of bracelets.  The WSOPA did not occur in 2011, but the series of tournaments resumed in 2012.

George Danzer | George Danzer (born 17 July 1983 in São Paulo, Brazil) is a German professional poker player, based in Salzburg, Austria who is a four-time World Series of Poker bracelets winner, winning the inaugural $10,000 Seven Card Razz Championship and then the $10,000 Seven-Card Stud High-Low Championship at the 2014 World Series of Poker.  He won his third bracelet winning the $5,000 Mixed Event 8-Game event at the 2014 World Series of Poker Asia Pacific.  His total live tournament winnings exceed $1.9 million

Michael Gathy | Michael Gathy is a professional poker player who won World Series of Poker bracelets at the 2012 and 2013 World Series of Poker.  He was the first World Series turbo event winner and the second Belgian bracelet winner.  He is one of two Belgians with multiple bracelets.

World Series of Poker multiple bracelet winners | Below is a list of all poker players who have won multiple World Series of Poker (WSOP) bracelets, together with the year(s) in which the bracelets were won.
hop1_titles: World Series of Poker multiple bracelet winners
response.summary_1: Evidence: The World Poker Tour "was started in 2002 ... by attorney/television producer Steven Lipscomb" (passage 1). Passage 3: David Chiu "is ... the winner of the 2008 World Poker Tour's WPT World Championship" and "has won five World Series of Poker bracelets" (also "born August 23, 1960"). Passage 5: George Danzer "is a ... who is a four-time World Series of Poker bracelets winner" and is "born July 17, 1983". Missing/bridge: none of the passages link a WPT winner to having exactly four WSOP bracelets. Next-hop direction: retrieve a list of World Poker Tour event champions and cross-reference each champion's World Series of Poker bracelet count to identify the champion with four WSOP bracelets, then retrieve that champion's birth date. Search anchors to use: "World Poker Tour winners list", "X World Poker Tour champion 'World Series of Poker bracelets' count", and the quoted phrases above.

He has won both a European Poker Tour (EPT) title and World Series of Poker bracelet, becoming the first person from the Netherlands to do so, first was at the EPT's inaugural Grand Final of the European Poker Tour in Monte Carlo in 2005 and then he won his first bracelet at the 2008 World Series of Poker in the $10,000 Limit Hold'em World Championship, becoming the first Dutch bracelet winner.
response.summary_2: John Hennigan — a World Poker Tour titleholder who has won four World Series of Poker bracelets — was born August 10, 1970. Passage 6 explicitly confirms he has four WSOP bracelets and a WPT title.
